title: Surface Delivery
subtitle: A compact starting shape for product surfaces that need design, implementation, verification, and tooling in one place.
scope: A feature or product surface moving from design intent through implementation and verification.
sourceCoverage: Template only; replace placeholder design, code, ticket, and verification references with real domain sources.

templateMeta.useWhen: Use this when one product surface needs design intent, implementation state, verification, and tooling to stay visible together.
templateMeta.operatingThesis: Treat one product surface as a joined delivery system: intent, implementation state, verification readiness, and tooling should stay visible together without collapsing into a single status.

semanticDefinition.objectiveRole: Make one product surface legible across intent, implementation state, verification readiness, and tooling.
semanticDefinition.templateObjective: Make one product surface legible across intent, implementation state, verification readiness, and the tools used to work on it.
semanticDefinition.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can identify the main surface, current implementation drift, immediate verification gaps, and which tools are used to evolve the work.

sections.role: Summarize delivery pressure and current readiness without carrying the detailed evidence.
Hold the product surface as a joined design, code, spec, interaction, and ticket entity.
Judge whether implementation targets satisfy the design and spec intent carried by the surface flow.
Track the checks that prove the aligned implementation is ready enough to ship or continue.
Expose the scripts, workflows, and local tools used to inspect, evolve, and verify the surface.

relationships.rationale: Status is only meaningful when it summarizes the actual surface flow being delivered.
Alignment rows should judge the design/code/spec entities named in the surface flow.

evidence.description: An alignment row should identify the surface-flow card, Figma node, or code path it judges.

relationships.rationale: Implementation alignment is not delivery readiness until checks exist for the aligned behavior.
Verification checkpoints need an operational tool path so a future session can rerun or extend them.

stageSignals.question: What first real surface, spec, code path, and ticket make this delivery doc tangible?

stageSignals.when: design-code-spec-flow exists but design-implementation-alignment has no corresponding rows
stageSignals.question: Which surface-flow cards lack an implementation-alignment judgment?

stageSignals.when: alignment rows are partial/current but verification-checkpoints do not cover the same surface concerns
stageSignals.question: Which verification checkpoint would make the current implementation alignment actionable?

stageSignals.when: status-snapshot or alignment state is older than source/code/check updates
stageSignals.question: What changed since the last delivery pass, and which status should move?

stageSignals.when: surface flow, alignment, verification, and tooling are all populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Is the surface shippable, blocked, or only visually/partially complete?

validOperations.label: Add surface flow card
validOperations.description: Add the first source-backed surface card with design/spec/code/ticket references.

validOperations.label: Add alignment row
validOperations.description: Add or update an alignment row for a surface-flow entity that lacks implementation judgment.

validOperations.label: Add verification checkpoint
validOperations.description: Add a check that proves or bounds a current alignment claim.

validOperations.label: Lower readiness status
validOperations.description: Lower or qualify status when verification does not support the delivery claim.

validOperations.label: Link tooling to check
validOperations.description: Link the script, workflow, or local tool that can run or inspect a verification checkpoint.

objective: Make one product surface legible across intent, implementation state, verification readiness, and the tools used to work on it.
successCondition: A reader can identify the main surface, current implementation drift, immediate verification gaps, and which tools are used to evolve the work.

callouts.title: How to use this template
items: Keep the section structure if your domain is primarily about shipping a surface from design to verified behavior.
Replace placeholder references before sharing the rendered HTML as an operational snapshot.

callout.title: Template intent
items: Use this opening section to summarize what exists and where the current delivery pressure is.
Keep it short; the detailed sections below carry the evidence.

data.name: Primary surface
notes: Start with the main surface that should be true when the feature is considered real.
Keep design, code, spec, and tracked interaction references together in one card.

data.name: Fallback and empty state
notes: Include recovery and empty-state surfaces early so they do not disappear behind the main happy path.
Main layout exists, but interaction states and validation affordances still drift from design.
Keep one explicit row for each important drift or missing implementation target.

data.name: Smoke path for primary submit
notes: Add the smallest trustworthy check first, then expand into a richer verification surface when the feature matures.

data.name: Empty state recovery check
notes: Use the checkpoint section when you need a compact list of checks rather than a full evidence-bearing verification model.

data.name: Universal renderer
data.purpose: Render the canonical JSON document into a standalone HTML snapshot with the full compositor embedded.
data.whenToUse: Use whenever you need a shareable artifact or want to refresh an existing rendered living doc.
notes: Use tooling sections for operational instruments, not for source material.
caveats: Rendered HTML is a snapshot and can drift from canonical JSON over time.

data.name: Registry overview generator
data.purpose: Expose the current convergence-type language so contributors can decide whether the doc needs a new section type or just a new section instance.
data.whenToUse: Use when the delivery surface starts needing structure that does not fit the existing type vocabulary.
caveats: Do not invent renderer behavior when a new type belongs in the registry.
